statement.en: DOES MILITARY SPENDING STIMULATE ENERGY CONSUMPTION IN NIGERIA?
statement.summary: Abstract Numerous studies have underscored the detrimental environmental effects of military spending vis-à-vis energy consumption, including pollution and habitat damage. Sequel to this, this study examines the effects of military spending on energy consumption in Nigeria. The data for the study were obtained from World Development Indicator (WDI, 2022) which covered from 1990-2022. The ARDL methods were employed to analyze the data. The analysis indicates that military spending has a positive effect on Nigeria's energy consumption in both the short and long term. An increase in military spending by a certain percentage is associated with a rise in energy consumption of approximately 0.18% in the short term and around 0.19% in the long term. The results demonstrate statistical significanc

reiTyping.dfumt8Reason: The axiom presents empirical findings from a single-country ARDL study (Nigeria, 1990-2022) on military spending → energy consumption causality. The claim is provisionally supported by specified methodology but lacks: (1) peer review status confirmation, (2) robustness checks across alternative specifications, (3) causal identification justification (ARDL assumes cointegration; military spending direction unclear), (4) generalizability bounds. The abstract appears incomplete ('statistical significanc'). This is preprint-stage empirical work requiring validation.

ramanujanFormula.interpretation: 軍事費の増加は、現在のエネルギー渇望と過去の力の蓄積の両方を呼び起こす。
